# listell.co.uk Holding Page — Infrastructure Reference

Manually provisioned via AWS CLI on 2026-02-27. To be migrated to Terraform.

---

## Resources Created

### S3 Bucket

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | `listell-co-uk-origin` |
| Region | `eu-west-2` |
| ARN | `arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-origin` |
| Public access | Blocked (all four settings) |
| Bucket policy | OAC-scoped `s3:GetObject` (see below) |

Content: `index.html` — simple dark holding page ("listell / coming soon").

### ACM Certificate

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| ARN | `arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:552644939129:certificate/1dee6ecf-3994-4f24-a948-8b921f93d39b` |
| Region | `us-east-1` (required for CloudFront) |
| Domains | `listell.co.uk`, `www.listell.co.uk` |
| Validation | DNS (CNAME records in Route 53) |
| Status | ISSUED |

### CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| ID | `E3O8ZSCPXMMUFI` |
| Name | `listell-co-uk-oac` |
| Signing protocol | `sigv4` |
| Signing behaviour | `always` |
| Origin type | `s3` |

### CloudFront Distribution

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| ID | `E1B1CZ81Y7ND1M` |
| ARN | `arn:aws:cloudfront::552644939129:distribution/E1B1CZ81Y7ND1M` |
| Domain | `dci395p49pi04.cloudfront.net` |
| Aliases | `listell.co.uk`, `www.listell.co.uk` |
| Origin | `listell-co-uk-origin.s3.eu-west-2.amazonaws.com` |
| OAC | `E3O8ZSCPXMMUFI` |
| Default root object | `index.html` |
| Viewer protocol | `redirect-to-https` |
| TLS minimum | `TLSv1.2_2021` |
| SSL method | `sni-only` |
| HTTP versions | `http2and3` |
| IPv6 | Enabled |
| Price class | `PriceClass_100` (US + Europe) |
| Cache policy | `658327ea-f89d-4fab-a63d-7e88639e58f6` (CachingOptimized, managed) |

### S3 Bucket Policy

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowCloudFrontOAC",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "s3:GetObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-origin/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::552644939129:distribution/E1B1CZ81Y7ND1M"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Route 53

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Hosted zone | `listell.co.uk` |
| Zone ID | `Z0437334HZSV61L914JE` |

Records created:

| Name | Type | Target |
|---|---|---|
| `listell.co.uk` | A (Alias) | `dci395p49pi04.cloudfront.net` (zone `Z2FDTNDATAQYW2`) |
| `listell.co.uk` | AAAA (Alias) | `dci395p49pi04.cloudfront.net` (zone `Z2FDTNDATAQYW2`) |
| `www.listell.co.uk` | A (Alias) | `dci395p49pi04.cloudfront.net` (zone `Z2FDTNDATAQYW2`) |
| `www.listell.co.uk` | AAAA (Alias) | `dci395p49pi04.cloudfront.net` (zone `Z2FDTNDATAQYW2`) |
| `_1350184d18c7f2ca546334fafefcc6cd.listell.co.uk` | CNAME | `_004059e10d8c1bf53278eb717be681f9.jkddzztszm.acm-validations.aws.` (ACM validation) |
| `_29fa1767377e63042a5354b4a8f60ded.www.listell.co.uk` | CNAME | `_81de6a6df5ff632dbf5aec9c4d5a1fce.jkddzztszm.acm-validations.aws.` (ACM validation) |

---

## Terraform Migration Notes

When converting to Terraform, the Route 53 zone is pre-existing — use a `data` source:

```hcl
data "aws_route53_zone" "listell" {
  name = "listell.co.uk."
}
```

Key points:

- **ACM cert must be provisioned in `us-east-1`** — use a provider alias
- **Cache policy** `658327ea-f89d-4fab-a63d-7e88639e58f6` is the AWS-managed `CachingOptimized` policy — reference via `data "aws_cloudfront_cache_policy"`
- **OAC** replaces the legacy OAI pattern — use `aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control`
- **S3 bucket policy** depends on the CloudFront distribution ARN — use implicit dependency via `aws_cloudfront_distribution.*.arn`
- **ACM validation records** can be automated with `aws_acm_certificate_validation` + `aws_route53_record` using `for_each` over `certificate.domain_validation_options`
- The `index.html` content can be managed with `aws_s3_object`

---

## AWS Account

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Account ID | `552644939129` |
| IAM user | `listellm` |

questions.question: Where should the Terraform live in this repo?

options.label: terraform/ at repo root (Recommended)
options.description: Standard flat layout — keeps it simple for a single-purpose repo

options.label: terraform/holding-page/
options.description: Nested under a subdirectory in case more stacks are added later

content.text:
Done. Here's the layout:

```
infra/
├── providers.tf    — AWS provider (eu-west-2) + us_east_1 alias; TF Cloud backend placeholder
├── locals.tf       — domain names, bucket name, CF hosted zone ID
├── s3.tf           — bucket, public access block, SSE, OAC bucket policy, index.html object
├── cloudfront.tf   — OAC, distribution (cache policy via data source)
├── acm.tf          — certificate (us_east_1 provider) + certificate_validation
├── dns.tf          — Route 53 data source + 4 alias records + ACM validation CNAMEs
├── outputs.tf      — CF domain, distribution ID, bucket ARN, cert ARN
├── imports.tf      — import blocks for all 13 existing resources
└── files/
    └── index.html  — dark holding page
```

**Things to note before running `terraform plan`:**

1. **Backend** — fill in the TF Cloud org/workspace in `providers.tf` before init
2. **SSE** — the original bucket had no SSE; Terraform will plan to add `AES256`. That's intentional.
3. **ACM import** — `aws_acm_certificate_validation` import may produce a no-op or minor diff; the cert is already `ISSUED` so no real change will occur
4. **ACM validation CNAME import IDs** — the double-underscore format (`Z0437334HZSV61L914JE__1350...`) is correct; the provider splits on first/last `_` to find zone ID and record type respectively

---

Now for the README — let me read it first.

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me summarize the differences:

**Thaler standard:**
1. **versions.tf** — separate file for `terraform {}` block with `required_version` and `required_providers`
   - Root: `required_version = ">= 1.14.0"`, provider `">= 6.0.0, < 7.0.0"`
   - Module: `required_version = ">= 1.10.0"`, provider `">= 6.0.0, < 7.0.0"` with `configuration_aliases = [aws.us_east_1]`


2. **providers.tf** — contains only provider blocks with `default_tags` configured
3. **backend.tf** — isolated backend configuration using S3 with encryption and lockfile enabled

**Current listellm:**
1. **providers.tf** — combines the terraform block and provider blocks in one file, lacks `default_tags`, and pins AWS provider to `~> 5.0` with `required_version >= 1.5.0`
2. **backend.tf** — exists but appears incomplete

The backend configuration is missing encryption and lockfile settings. I need to create a separate versions file with updated version constraints, move the terraform block out of providers.tf, add default tags to both providers with project and management metadata, update the backend with the missing security settings, and remove redundant inline tags from resources since default tags will handle the common ones.

Now I'm realizing that if I apply default tags with the lowercase "terraform" value for ManagedBy, I should strip out the capitalized versions from individual resources. Looking at the current setup, both s3.tf and cloudfront.tf have `ManagedBy = "Terraform"` with a capital T, which won't match the default tag approach. I'll need to remove those duplicates while preserving the Name tags that are resource-specific.

My plan is to create a new versions.tf file and refactor providers.tf to include the default_tags block with the correct lowercase values, then clean up the redundant tags from the individual resource files.

I also need to update backend.tf to add the encrypt flag and switch to the newer use_lockfile mechanism instead of the older dynamodb_table approach for state locking. I'm noting that `use_lockfile = true` requires Terraform >= 1.10 and S3 provider >= 6.0, which aligns with the planned upgrade to >= 1.14.0, so that's compatible. The backend bucket itself may or may not exist, but that's outside the scope of what we're adjusting here. I should verify the Terraform version constraint though—1.14.0 seems quite aggressive given that 1.9.x was the latest stable release as of my last update, so I may need to reconsider that requirement. Looking at the thaler module pattern, I can see that child modules use `configuration_aliases` for provider aliases, but since listellm/infra is a root module without child modules, I don't need that in my versions.tf. Let me verify this approach by checking if there are other root-level modules that follow the same pattern without aliases.

Ah I see. The provider binary at `/home/listellm/.vscode/extensions/hashicorp.terraform-2.37.7-linux-x64/bin/terraform-ls serve` (terraform-ls) is using the provider binary at pid 1539005 (`.terraform/providers/registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws/6.34.0/linux_amd64/terraform-provider-aws_v6.34.0_x5`). This is the VS Code Terraform extension doing language server operations.

The issue is that the VS Code terraform-ls is running the provider in the plugin cache, and Terraform is trying to install/use the same binary. 

The `text file busy` error occurs because that binary is currently executing as a process, so Terraform can't overwrite or access it. I can work around this by retrying `terraform init` after a pause, disabling the plugin cache with `TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR=""`, or using `-plugin-dir` to point elsewhere.

Looking at process 1539005, the path shows `.terraform/providers/registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws/6.34.0/linux_amd64/terraform-provider-aws_v6.34.0_x5` - it's in the local `.terraform` directory, not the global plugin cache. The provider binary is likely being held open by terraform-ls. The simplest fix is to either wait for the VS Code extension to release the lock, or bypass the cache entirely during this init operation.

Rather than killing processes without consent, I'll just retry the init - the file lock should clear quickly once the language server finishes its validation.

content.text: The VS Code Terraform extension (`terraform-ls`) is holding the provider binary open (pid `1539005`). That's the "text file busy" cause. It will release once the language server finishes its current operation — just retry init.

**Error:** `resource aws_acm_certificate_validation doesn't support import` — I need to remove that import block.

The import block for `aws_acm_certificate_validation.listell` needs to be removed since this resource type doesn't support import. The `aws_acm_certificate_validation` resource is a "waiter" resource in Terraform — it just polls until the certificate is validated. 

Since the cert is already ISSUED, Terraform will just see it as already validated and do nothing. Let me remove that import block from `imports.tf` and review the plan output more carefully to verify the expected changes across the certificate, CloudFront distribution, S3 bucket, and access block resources.

The remaining changes look good — the new server-side encryption configuration and the S3 object updates with source and tags are all expected. I'll go ahead and remove that problematic `aws_acm_certificate_validation` import block.

content.thinking:
The lock file was generated with only local checksums (since we used `-plugin-dir`), and now Terraform is complaining that the installed package doesn't match the lock file checksums. This happens because the local copy is "unauthenticated" - the checksum in the lock file was computed from the local file, but the expected format is the official registry checksum.

The fix is to update the lock file with the correct checksums from the registry. I can do this with:
```
terraform providers lock
```

This will fetch the checksums from the registry and update the lock file. But we might need to do this online.


Actually, I could just re-run the init command with the upgrade flag to regenerate the lock file entirely, though I'd run into the same "text file busy" error unless the previous process has fully terminated. Let me verify whether that process is still active.

content.thinking:
Plan ran cleanly! Let me analyze the results:

**13 to import, 2 to add, 8 to change, 0 to destroy** — this is clean.

Let me go through each change:

1. **`aws_acm_certificate.listell`** — update in-place: add tags (`Name`, `ManagedBy`, `Project`) ✅ expected

2. **`aws_acm_certificate_validation.listell`** — create ✅ expected (not imported, just created as a waiter resource; cert is already ISSUED so this will complete immediately)
   
3. **`aws_cloudfront_distribution.listell`** — there are a few attribute mismatches here. The comment will be removed since we didn't specify one in our config, which is fine. More importantly, compression is currently enabled on the existing distribution but defaults to false in Terraform, so it wants to disable it — I should explicitly set `compress = true` to keep the current behavior and avoid unnecessary changes.

The origin ID is shifting from the manually-created `"listell-co-uk-s3"` to `"listell-co-uk-origin"` (from our S3 bucket resource), which is a real functional change but shouldn't break anything since the OAC reference is tied to the origin config itself. We're also adding tags as expected.

4. **`aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control.listell`** — the description is being reset to the default `"Managed by Terraform"` instead of the original `"OAC for listell.co.uk S3 origin"`. I should add the description to our config if we want to preserve the original text.

5. **`aws_route53_record.acm_validation["listell.co.uk"]`** and **`aws_route53_record.acm_validation["www.listell.co.uk"]`** — both have TTL changes from 300 to 60. Our config specifies 60, but the existing records are at 300. Since ACM validation doesn't require a specific TTL, I could either match the existing 300 to avoid the change or keep 60 since it doesn't affect validation.

6. **`aws_s3_bucket.origin`** — tags are being updated as expected.

7. **`aws_s3_bucket_policy.origin`** — the policy will be reapplied since it's generated from a data source, which will show as a known change after apply.

8. **`aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.origin`** — this is a new resource being created, which is the expected security improvement.

9. **`aws_s3_object.index`** — adding source, source_hash, and tags as expected.

I need to address two configuration gaps: adding `compress = true` to the CloudFront default cache behavior to match the existing setting, and including the OAC description "OAC for listell.co.uk S3 origin" in the config instead of letting it default to the Terraform-managed label. There's also a CloudFront distribution comment that was manually added.

The TTL change on ACM validation records from 300 to 60 seconds is minor and won't affect functionality, so I'll keep it at 60. Overall this is a clean migration with zero resources being destroyed and all changes being either expected improvements or benign normalizations. Before applying, I just need to add those two missing configuration details to the CloudFront resources.

Looking at the OAC description change, the plan wants to replace "OAC for listell.co.uk S3 origin" with "Managed by Terraform" — that's just the default value when no description is specified. I should preserve the existing description by setting it explicitly. These are the key issues worth flagging to the user: the compress setting potentially being disabled on CloudFront, and the description changes. I'll present these findings and offer to fix them before proceeding.

content.text:
Plan is clean — **13 import, 2 add, 8 change, 0 destroy**. All good except two things worth fixing before apply:

**1. `compress = true → false` on the CloudFront default cache behaviour** — the existing distribution has compression enabled; our config didn't include `compress`, so Terraform defaults it to `false`. That would disable gzip/Brotli serving from CloudFront.

**2. OAC description drift** — existing value is `"OAC for listell.co.uk S3 origin"`; our config omitted the attribute so Terraform will overwrite with `"Managed by Terraform"`.

The TTL change on the ACM validation CNAMEs (300 → 60) is harmless for a cert that's already ISSUED — leaving it.

Let me fix both:

input.new_string:
resource "aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control" "listell" {
  name                              = "listell-co-uk-oac"
  description                       = "OAC for listell.co.uk S3 origin"
  origin_access_control_origin_type = "s3"
  signing_behavior                  = "always"
  signing_protocol                  = "sigv4"
}

ci.yml
claude-code-review.yml
deploy-app.yml
deploy-infra.yml

1→name: Deploy Infrastructure
     2→
     3→on:
     4→  push:
     5→    branches: [main]
     6→    paths:
     7→      - infra/**
     8→  pull_request:
     9→    branches: [main]
    10→    paths:
    11→      - infra/**
    12→  workflow_dispatch:
    13→    inputs:
    14→      targets:
    15→        description: 'Optional Terraform targets, e.g. "-target=module.database -target=module.ecr"'
    16→        required: false
    17→        default: ''
    18→
    19→permissions:
    20→  contents: read
    21→  id-token: write
    22→  pull-requests: write
    23→
    24→jobs:
    25→  lint:
    26→    name: Terraform Lint
    27→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    28→    defaults:
    29→      run:
    30→        working-directory: infra
    31→    steps:
    32→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    33→
    34→      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
    35→        with:
    36→          terraform_version: "1.10.0"
    37→
    38→      - name: Terraform fmt check
    39→        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
    40→
    41→      - uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v6
    42→        with:
    43→          tflint_version: v0.61.0
    44→
    45→      - uses: actions/cache@v4
    46→        with:
    47→          path: ~/.tflint.d/plugins
    48→          key: ${{ runner.os }}-tflint-${{ hashFiles('.config/.tflint.hcl') }}
    49→
    50→      - name: tflint init
    51→        run: tflint --init --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl
    52→
    53→      - name: tflint
    54→        run: tflint --recursive --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl
    55→
    56→      - name: trivy
    57→        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.34.1

file.content:
name: Deploy Infrastructure

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - infra/**
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - infra/**
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      targets:
        description: 'Optional Terraform targets, e.g. "-target=module.database -target=module.ecr"'
        required: false
        default: ''

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  lint:
    name: Terraform Lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.10.0"

      - name: Terraform fmt check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive

      - uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v6
        with:
          tflint_version: v0.61.0

      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.tflint.d/plugins
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-tflint-${{ hashFiles('.config/.tflint.hcl') }}

      - name: tflint init
        run: tflint --init --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl

      - name: tflint
        run: tflint --recursive --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl

      - name: trivy
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.34.1
        with:
          scan-type: config
          scan-ref: infra
          skip-dirs: "**/.terraform"
          trivyignores: .config/.trivyignore

      - name: checkov
        uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@v12.1347.0
        with:
          directory: infra
          config_file: .config/.checkov.yaml

  plan:
    name: Terraform Plan
    needs: [lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/thaler-github-actions
          aws-region: eu-west-2

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.14.0"

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars 2>&1 | tee plan-output.txt
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Post plan as PR comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('infra/plan-output.txt', 'utf8');
            const truncated = plan.length > 60000
              ? plan.substring(0, 60000) + '\n...(truncated)'
              : plan;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `## Terraform Plan\n\`\`\`\n${truncated}\n\`\`\``
            });

      - name: Fail if plan failed
        if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
        run: exit 1

  apply:
    name: Terraform Apply
    needs: [lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: (github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main') || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/thaler-github-actions
          aws-region: eu-west-2

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.14.0"

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Apply
        env:
          TF_VAR_aws_lambda_function_presignup_allowed_email: ${{ secrets.PRESIGNUP_ALLOWED_EMAIL }}
          TF_VAR_aws_cognito_identity_provider_google_client_id: ${{ secrets.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID }}
          TF_VAR_aws_cognito_identity_provider_google_client_secret: ${{ secrets.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET }}
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars ${{ github.event.inputs.targets }}

file.content:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  api:
    name: API
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: api
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: api/go.mod

      - run: go mod download

      - run: go vet ./...

      - run: go build ./...

      - run: go test -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: api-coverage
          path: api/coverage.out

  web:
    name: Web
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - run: npm ci

      - run: npx svelte-kit sync

      - run: npm run check

      - run: npm test

      - run: npm run build

  functions:
    name: Functions
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: functions
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: functions/go.mod

      - run: go mod download

      - run: go vet ./...

      - run: go test -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...

  release:
    name: Release
    needs: [api, web, functions]
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      issues: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          persist-credentials: false

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: package-lock.json

      - run: npm ci

      - run: npx semantic-release
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

---
config {
  format     = "junit"
  plugin_dir = "~/.tflint.d/plugins"

  call_module_type    = "all"
  force               = false
  disabled_by_default = false

  ignore_module = {
  }
}

plugin "terraform" {
  enabled = true
  preset  = "recommended"
}

plugin "aws" {
  enabled = true
  version = "0.38.0"
  source  = "github.com/terraform-linters/tflint-ruleset-aws"
}

rule "terraform_comment_syntax" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_documented_outputs" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_documented_variables" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_naming_convention" {
  enabled = true
  format  = "snake_case"
}

rule "terraform_standard_module_structure" {
  enabled = false
}

rule "terraform_unused_required_providers" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_deprecated_index" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_deprecated_interpolation" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_unused_declarations" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_typed_variables" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_module_pinned_source" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_workspace_remote" {
  enabled = true
}
---
download-external-modules: false
external-modules-download-path: .external_modules
framework: terraform
skip-check:
  - CKV_TF_1 # https://github.com/bridgecrewio/checkov/issues/5286
  - CKV_TF_2
  # GitHub Actions OIDC role uses AdministratorAccess — intentional for personal project bootstrap
  - CKV_AWS_274  # AdministratorAccess policy attached
  - CKV_AWS_1    # IAM full admin privileges
  - CKV_AWS_110  # IAM privilege escalation (bootstrap role needs it)
  - CKV_AWS_109  # IAM permissions management
  - CKV_AWS_358  # OIDC org-specific trust (personal repo, single-owner)
  - CKV_AWS_108  # IAM data exfiltration
  - CKV_AWS_49   # IAM wildcard actions (bootstrap role)
  - CKV_AWS_107  # IAM credentials exposure
  - CKV_AWS_356  # IAM wildcard resources (bootstrap role)
  - CKV_AWS_111  # IAM write without constraints (bootstrap role)
  - CKV_AWS_283  # IAM all principals
  - CKV_AWS_60   # IAM assume role constraints
  - CKV_AWS_61   # IAM assume role across services
  - CKV_AWS_62   # IAM full admin
  - CKV_AWS_63   # IAM wildcard actions
  - CKV_AWS_234  # ACM logging (very new check, not yet widely supported)
  - CKV_AWS_216  # CloudFront enabled check (false positive — it is enabled)
  # CloudWatch alarm without actions — intentional for personal app (no SNS topic configured)
  - CKV_AWS_319  # CloudWatch alarm actions enabled
  # Lambda not publicly accessible — no function URL exists, false positive
  - CKV_AWS_301  # Lambda publicly accessible check
  # CloudFront — WAF, logging, geo-restriction, origin failover overkill for personal portfolio
  - CKV_AWS_68    # CloudFront WAF
  - CKV2_AWS_47   # CloudFront WAFv2 Log4j AMR (also WAF)
  - CKV_AWS_86    # CloudFront access logging
  - CKV_AWS_310   # CloudFront origin failover
  - CKV_AWS_374   # CloudFront geo restriction
  # Lambda — DLQ, VPC, X-Ray, code-signing, concurrency, KMS env vars overkill for personal app
  - CKV_AWS_50    # Lambda X-Ray tracing
  - CKV_AWS_115   # Lambda function-level concurrency limit
  - CKV_AWS_116   # Lambda DLQ
  - CKV_AWS_117   # Lambda in VPC
  - CKV_AWS_173   # Lambda env var KMS encryption
  - CKV_AWS_272   # Lambda code-signing
  # CloudWatch — KMS encryption and 1-year retention overkill for personal app
  - CKV_AWS_158   # CW log group KMS encryption
  - CKV_AWS_338   # CW log group 1-year retention (14 days is sufficient here)
  # DynamoDB — CMK and PITR overkill for personal app
  - CKV_AWS_28    # DynamoDB PITR
  - CKV_AWS_119   # DynamoDB CMK encryption
  # ECR — CMK encryption overkill; mutable tags intentional (latest overwritten on each deploy)
  - CKV_AWS_136   # ECR KMS encryption
  - CKV_AWS_51    # ECR image tag immutability — intentional MUTABLE for latest tag overwrite
  # S3 static website — CMK, versioning, logging, replication, lifecycle, events all overkill
  - CKV_AWS_18    # S3 access logging
  - CKV_AWS_21    # S3 versioning
  - CKV_AWS_144   # S3 cross-region replication
  - CKV_AWS_145   # S3 KMS encryption by default
  - CKV2_AWS_61   # S3 lifecycle policy
  - CKV2_AWS_62   # S3 event notifications
  # Step Functions — X-Ray and full execution history logging overkill for personal app
  - CKV_AWS_284   # Step Functions X-Ray
  - CKV_AWS_285   # Step Functions execution history logging (ERROR level is configured)
  # EventBridge — CMK overkill for personal app
  - CKV_AWS_297   # EventBridge Scheduler CMK
  # API Gateway — route authorisation added in Phase 2 when Cognito is wired in
  - CKV_AWS_309   # API GW route authorisation type
  # CloudFront — strict security headers policy; overkill for personal portfolio
  - CKV2_AWS_32   # CloudFront strict security headers policy
  # Route53 — false positive; A records do alias CloudFront distributions
  - CKV2_AWS_23   # Route53 A record attached resource
  # S3 — false positive in older checkov; SSE configured via aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration
  - CKV_AWS_19    # S3 encrypted at rest (inline block check; separate resource is present)
---
# CloudFront WAF — overkill for a personal portfolio app
AVD-AWS-0011

# CloudFront access logging — not required for personal use
AVD-AWS-0010

# S3 static website bucket — logging not required for personal use
AVD-AWS-0089

# S3 static website bucket — versioning not appropriate for a static build artifact
AVD-AWS-0090

# S3 static website bucket — CMK encryption overkill; AES256 SSE sufficient
AVD-AWS-0132

# CloudWatch log groups — CMK encryption overkill for a personal app; default AES256 is sufficient
AVD-AWS-0017

# Lambda X-Ray tracing — not required for a personal portfolio app
AVD-AWS-0066

# DynamoDB PITR disabled — intentional for Phase 1/2; not yet needed for a personal app
AVD-AWS-0024

# DynamoDB default AWS-managed encryption — CMK overkill for a personal project
AVD-AWS-0025

# ECR repositories — CMK encryption overkill for a personal project; AWS-managed encryption sufficient
AVD-AWS-0033

# ECR mutable tags — intentional; deploy-app.yml overwrites :latest on each push
AVD-AWS-0031

content.thinking:
Now I have a full picture of the thaler workflow structure and config files. Let me plan what to create for listellm:

**Key differences from thaler:**
1. **No modules** — listellm/infra is a flat root module, no child modules
2. **No var-file** — no `environments/prod.tfvars` (listellm has no variables)
3. **No app code** — just infra, no CI for app
4. **Same OIDC auth pattern** — need to assume an IAM role via OIDC
5. **Terraform version** — `1.14.0` (matching thaler and our `versions.tf`)


6. **Workflow files needed** — deploy-infra.yml for lint, plan, and apply stages
7. **Config files** — tflint, checkov, and trivyignore with adjustments for the flat structure and CloudFront/S3 resources

Since there are no variables, the workflow won't need a var-file parameter, and I can simplify the terraform apply step accordingly.

For the plan output, I need to be careful with the file path — since the working directory is set to `infra`, the `plan-output.txt` file will be created relative to that directory, so I should reference it correctly in subsequent steps.

For tflint and checkov, I'll mirror thaler's configuration but adjust for listellm's simpler setup, keeping the same tool versions and disabling unnecessary checks like the module structure rule since we're using a flat root module.

Now I'm reviewing which security checks are actually relevant for our infrastructure—CloudFront and S3 need WAF, logging, and encryption configurations, while Route53 and S3 SSE have false positives we can ignore. I'll skip all the Lambda, DynamoDB, and other service-specific checks that thaler uses since we don't have those components.

For the IAM role, I need to create a new `listellm-github-actions` role in AWS to replace thaler's, though I should note that this role creation is a prerequisite step. I'm noticing a version mismatch between the lint and plan/apply stages—lint uses `1.10.0` for the format check while plan/apply uses `1.14.0`, which aligns with the root module's `required_version`. Since the format check needs to validate against the actual Terraform version constraints, I should standardize on `1.14.0` for consistency, even though thaler's setup uses `1.10.0` for their lower module minimum.

Now I'm planning out the configuration files I need to create: the GitHub Actions workflow, tflint config, checkov config, and trivy ignore file. For the checkov skips, I'm carrying over the same rules from thaler—the Terraform registry pinning checks and the bootstrap IAM role exceptions.

Looking at what checks are actually relevant to our current infrastructure, I should focus on the S3 bucket, CloudFront distribution, and ACM certificate rather than including skips for things we might add later. I'll keep the checkov configuration tight and only include what applies to what we're deploying now. M certificate and Route53 records are the main pieces here. I've already got inline checkov skips for CloudFront, S3, and Route53 to handle the false positives and features we're intentionally not implementing. For the OIDC role, I need to use the secrets variable for the AWS account ID instead of hardcoding it, and name the role `listell-github-actions`. Before I start writing the files, I should check whether the listellm repo already has a `.github/` or `.config/` directory.

1. `.github/workflows/deploy-infra.yml`
2. `.config/.tflint.hcl`
3. `.config/.checkov.yaml`4. `.config/.trivyignore`

- IAM role: `listell-github-actions`

For the plan step, there's one gotcha from thaler: the `plan-output.txt` is read from `infra/plan-output.txt` in the github-script step (runs from workspace root). 

Since our working-directory is also `infra`, this should work the same way. Now I'll set up all the config files. For tflint, I'm keeping thaler's config as-is since it's solid—the `terraform_standard_module_structure` rule is disabled there, which makes sense for us too since we don't have the full module structure with README files in the infra directory. For checkov, I'll adapt their configuration to fit our needs.

The inline `#checkov:skip` annotations in our Terraform files already handle resource-specific checks like S3 and CloudFront, and the checkov-action respects these when running. So I only need to add global skips to `.checkov.yaml` for checks that aren't tied to specific resources—mainly the module source pinning rules since we're not using registry modules, and potentially the IAM bootstrap role check if we add an OIDC role.

Beyond those, I'm identifying additional checks to skip globally: ACM logging, the Route53 A record false positive, the S3 SSE false positive that's already handled elsewhere, and then pulling in the relevant CloudFront and S3 checks from our existing skip list.

Since most resource-level checks already have inline `checkov:skip` annotations in the Terraform files, the `.checkov.yaml` configuration should focus on directory-level rules that can't be inlined—so I'll keep it aligned with what we had before but remove anything that doesn't apply to our actual infrastructure like Lambda, DynamoDB, ECR, and the other services we're not using.

I don't need to add a disable for undocumented variables since we have no variables in the root module anyway, and our outputs already have descriptions, so that's covered.

input.content:
name: Deploy Infrastructure

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - infra/**
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - infra/**
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      targets:
        description: 'Optional Terraform targets, e.g. "-target=aws_cloudfront_distribution.listell"'
        required: false
        default: ''

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  lint:
    name: Terraform Lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.14.0"

      - name: Terraform fmt check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive

      - uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v6
        with:
          tflint_version: v0.61.0

      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.tflint.d/plugins
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-tflint-${{ hashFiles('.config/.tflint.hcl') }}

      - name: tflint init
        run: tflint --init --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl

      - name: tflint
        run: tflint --recursive --config=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.config/.tflint.hcl

      - name: trivy
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@0.34.1
        with:
          scan-type: config
          scan-ref: infra
          skip-dirs: "**/.terraform"
          trivyignores: .config/.trivyignore

      - name: checkov
        uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@v12.1347.0
        with:
          directory: infra
          config_file: .config/.checkov.yaml

  plan:
    name: Terraform Plan
    needs: [lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/listell-github-actions
          aws-region: eu-west-2

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.14.0"

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color 2>&1 | tee plan-output.txt
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Post plan as PR comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('infra/plan-output.txt', 'utf8');
            const truncated = plan.length > 60000
              ? plan.substring(0, 60000) + '\n...(truncated)'
              : plan;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `## Terraform Plan\n\`\`\`\n${truncated}\n\`\`\``
            });

      - name: Fail if plan failed
        if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
        run: exit 1

  apply:
    name: Terraform Apply
    needs: [lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: (github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main') || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ secrets.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/listell-github-actions
          aws-region: eu-west-2

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.14.0"

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Apply
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve ${{ github.event.inputs.targets }}

content.text:
Done. Files created:

```
.config/
├── .checkov.yaml
├── .tflint.hcl
└── .trivyignore
.github/workflows/
└── deploy-infra.yml
```

**How it works (identical pattern to thaler):**

| Trigger | Jobs run |
|---|---|
| PR targeting `main` (paths: `infra/**`) | `lint` → `plan` (posts output as PR comment) |
| Push to `main` (paths: `infra/**`) | `lint` → `apply` |
| `workflow_dispatch` | `lint` → `apply` (optional `-target` input) |

**One prerequisite before this will work:** the `listell-github-actions` IAM role needs to exist in account `552644939129` with an OIDC trust policy allowing `repo:listellm/listellm:*`. You'll also need a `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` repository secret set to `552644939129`. The role itself is the same bootstrap pattern as thaler — worth adding to the infra Terraform if you want it managed as code.

file.content:
repos:

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v6.0.0
    hooks:
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-added-large-files
        args:
          - '--maxkb=2048'
          - '--enforce-all'
      - id: check-case-conflict
      - id: check-json
      - id: check-yaml
      - id: detect-aws-credentials
        args:
          - '--allow-missing-credentials'
      - id: detect-private-key
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: check-shebang-scripts-are-executable

  - repo: https://github.com/adrienverge/yamllint.git
    rev: v1.37.1
    hooks:
      - id: yamllint
        args: [-c=./.config/.yamllint.yaml]

  - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
    rev: v1.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-secrets
        args: ['--baseline', '.config/.secrets.baseline']
        exclude: .config/.secrets.baseline

  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: prettier
        name: prettier
        entry: web/node_modules/.bin/prettier --write --ignore-unknown
          --plugin=web/node_modules/prettier-plugin-svelte/plugin.js
        language: system
        files: ^web/.*\.(ts|js|svelte|css|json)$

  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.9.9
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
        files: ^price-fetcher/
      - id: ruff-format
        files: ^price-fetcher/

  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: go-fmt
        name: go fmt
        entry: gofmt -l -w
        language: system
        types: [go]
        files: ^api/

      - id: golangci-lint
        name: golangci-lint
        entry: bash -c 'cd api && golangci-lint run'
        language: system
        types: [go]
        pass_filenames: false
        files: ^api/

  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.98.1
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1

      - id: terraform_validate
        files: ^infra/
        exclude: ^infra/modules/frontend/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1

      - id: terraform_docs
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - '--args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.terraform-docs.yml'

      - id: terraform_tflint
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1
          - --hook-config=--delegate-chdir
          - '--args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.tflint.hcl'

      - id: terraform_trivy
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1
          - --args=--skip-dirs="**/.terraform"
          - '--args=--ignorefile=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.trivyignore'

  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.0
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks

  - repo: https://github.com/bridgecrewio/checkov.git
    rev: 3.2.266
    hooks:
      - id: checkov
        args:
          - '-d infra'
          - '--config-file=.config/.checkov.yaml'

1→.PHONY: dev-db dev-api dev-web dev-setup fetch-prices test test-api test-web test-functions test-cover \
     2→        build-api-local build-api-arm64 build-presignup-local scan-api \
     3→        bootstrap-state tf-init tf-plan tf-apply tf-apply-bootstrap \
     4→        scan-price-fetcher build-price-fetcher-local invoke-price-fetcher
     5→
     6→dev-db:
     7→	docker compose up -d
     8→
     9→dev-api:
    10→	cd api && \
    11→	DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:8000 \
    12→	TABLE_NAME=thaler \
    13→	AWS_REGION=eu-west-2 \
    14→	AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=local \
    15→	AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=local \
    16→	go run ./cmd/api/...
    17→
    18→dev-web:
    19→	cd web && npm run dev
    20→
    21→dev-setup:
    22→	cd web && npm install
    23→	pip3 install -r price-fetcher/requirements.txt -q
    24→	docker compose up -d
    25→
    26→fetch-prices:
    27→	DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:8000 \
    28→	TABLE_NAME=thaler \
    29→	AWS_REGION=eu-west-2 \
    30→	AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=local \
    31→	AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=local \
    32→	python3 price-fetcher/fetcher.py
    33→
    34→test: test-api test-web test-functions
    35→
    36→test-api:
    37→	cd api && go test ./... -v
    38→
    39→test-web:
    40→	cd web && npm test
    41→
    42→test-functions:
    43→	cd functions && go test ./... -v
    44→
    45→test-cover:
    46→	cd api && go test ./... -coverprofile=coverage.out -covermode=atomic && go tool cover -func=coverage.out
    47→
    48→# Build for local testing (amd64, loads into local Docker daemon)
    49→build-api-local:
    50→	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-api:local api/
    51→
    52→# Build presignup Lambda for local testing (amd64, loads into local Docker daemon)
    53→build-presignup-local:
    54→	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-presignup:local -f functions/presignup/Dockerfile functions/
    55→
    56→# Build for ARM64 Lambda target (loads into local Docker daemon via QEMU)
    57→build-api-arm64:
    58→	docker buildx build --platform linux/arm64 --load -t thaler-api:arm64 api/
    59→
    60→# Scan local image for vulnerabilities (requires build-api-local first)
    61→scan-api: build-api-local
    62→	docker run --rm \
    63→		-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
    64→		aquasec/trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL thaler-api:local
    65→
    66→bootstrap-state:
    67→	scripts/bootstrap-state.sh
    68→
    69→tf-init:
    70→	cd infra && terraform init
    71→
    72→tf-plan:
    73→	cd infra && terraform plan -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars
    74→
    75→tf-apply:
    76→	cd infra && terraform apply -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars
    77→
    78→# Bootstrap apply: DynamoDB + ECR only (images must exist before full apply)
    79→tf-apply-bootstrap:
    80→	cd infra && terraform apply \
    81→		-target=module.database \
    82→		-target=module.ecr \
    83→		-var-file=environments/prod.tfvars
    84→
    85→build-price-fetcher-local:
    86→	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-price-fetcher:local price-fetcher/
    87→
    88→# Scan price fetcher image for vulnerabilities (requires build-price-fetcher-local first)
    89→scan-price-fetcher: build-price-fetcher-local
    90→	docker run --rm \
    91→		-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
    92→		aquasec/trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL thaler-price-fetcher:local
    93→
    94→invoke-price-fetcher:
    95→	aws lambda invoke \
    96→		--function-name thaler-price-fetcher \
    97→		--payload '{}' \
    98→		/tmp/price-fetcher-response.json && \
    99→	cat /tmp/price-fetcher-response.json
   100→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
.PHONY: dev-db dev-api dev-web dev-setup fetch-prices test test-api test-web test-functions test-cover \
        build-api-local build-api-arm64 build-presignup-local scan-api \
        bootstrap-state tf-init tf-plan tf-apply tf-apply-bootstrap \
        scan-price-fetcher build-price-fetcher-local invoke-price-fetcher

dev-db:
	docker compose up -d

dev-api:
	cd api && \
	DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:8000 \
	TABLE_NAME=thaler \
	AWS_REGION=eu-west-2 \
	AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=local \
	AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=local \
	go run ./cmd/api/...

dev-web:
	cd web && npm run dev

dev-setup:
	cd web && npm install
	pip3 install -r price-fetcher/requirements.txt -q
	docker compose up -d

fetch-prices:
	DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:8000 \
	TABLE_NAME=thaler \
	AWS_REGION=eu-west-2 \
	AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=local \
	AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=local \
	python3 price-fetcher/fetcher.py

test: test-api test-web test-functions

test-api:
	cd api && go test ./... -v

test-web:
	cd web && npm test

test-functions:
	cd functions && go test ./... -v

test-cover:
	cd api && go test ./... -coverprofile=coverage.out -covermode=atomic && go tool cover -func=coverage.out

# Build for local testing (amd64, loads into local Docker daemon)
build-api-local:
	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-api:local api/

# Build presignup Lambda for local testing (amd64, loads into local Docker daemon)
build-presignup-local:
	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-presignup:local -f functions/presignup/Dockerfile functions/

# Build for ARM64 Lambda target (loads into local Docker daemon via QEMU)
build-api-arm64:
	docker buildx build --platform linux/arm64 --load -t thaler-api:arm64 api/

# Scan local image for vulnerabilities (requires build-api-local first)
scan-api: build-api-local
	docker run --rm \
		-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
		aquasec/trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL thaler-api:local

bootstrap-state:
	scripts/bootstrap-state.sh

tf-init:
	cd infra && terraform init

tf-plan:
	cd infra && terraform plan -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars

tf-apply:
	cd infra && terraform apply -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars

# Bootstrap apply: DynamoDB + ECR only (images must exist before full apply)
tf-apply-bootstrap:
	cd infra && terraform apply \
		-target=module.database \
		-target=module.ecr \
		-var-file=environments/prod.tfvars

build-price-fetcher-local:
	docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --load -t thaler-price-fetcher:local price-fetcher/

# Scan price fetcher image for vulnerabilities (requires build-price-fetcher-local first)
scan-price-fetcher: build-price-fetcher-local
	docker run --rm \
		-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
		aquasec/trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL thaler-price-fetcher:local

invoke-price-fetcher:
	aws lambda invoke \
		--function-name thaler-price-fetcher \
		--payload '{}' \
		/tmp/price-fetcher-response.json && \
	cat /tmp/price-fetcher-response.json

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of everything. Let me plan what to create for listellm:

**Files to create:**

1. **`.gitignore`** — strip Go, Python, Node sections (not relevant); keep Terraform, env, editor, OS, Claude sections

2. **`.pre-commit-config.yaml`** — strip Go/Python/Node/Docker hooks; keep:
   - pre-commit-hooks (general)
   - yamllint
   - detect-secrets
   - gitleaks
   - terraform hooks (fmt, validate, docs, tflint, trivy)
   - checkov
   Note: Remove `terraform_validate` exclude for modules (we have no modules to exclude)
   
3. **`renovate.json`** — keep GitHub Actions and Terraform dependency management, removing language-specific package managers

4. **`Makefile`** — focus on Terraform operations like init, plan, and apply

5. **`.vscode/settings.json`** — configure for Terraform linting and formatting, stripping language-specific tools

6. **`.config/.yamllint.yaml`** — keep as-is

7. **`.config/.terraform-docs.yml`** — adjust the readme output path to point to the infra directory

Now I'm working through where terraform-docs should write its output. The config specifies `readme.md` as the target file, but since the infra code lives in its own directory, I need to determine whether this should generate at the repo root or within the infra folder. Looking at how the pre-commit hook runs terraform-docs, it executes relative to the module location, so outputting to `infra/readme.md` makes more sense for an infrastructure-only repository.

For the secrets baseline, I need to generate a fresh one for the listellm configuration using the detect-secrets tool. I'll create a clean baseline since there aren't any sensitive files to detect yet.

Now I'm mapping out the specific configuration files I need to set up. For `.gitignore`, I'll focus on Terraform, environment variables, editor settings, and OS files while removing language-specific patterns for Go, Node, and Python. The pre-commit hooks will stay mostly the same but swap out the language linters for Terraform-specific tools like terraform-docs, tflint, and trivy. Renovate needs to shift from npm and Go managers to Terraform provider updates. The Makefile simplifies to just the core Terraform commands, and VS Code settings...

Configuring editor settings...

The terraform-docs config points to the infra readme where the generated documentation will live, and I'm starting with a fresh secrets baseline. I should double-check the CLAUDE.md file from thaler to see if there's anything project-specific I'm missing. I should focus on the tooling files rather than getting sidetracked with project-specific documentation. Let me check what's in the `.docs` and `docs` directories in thaler, then move forward with creating the missing files. For `.secrets.baseline`, I'll generate a minimal clean baseline since that's straightforward. I'm also thinking through the `renovate.json` configuration and considering whether to add a terraform manager section for provider version bumps.

Writing the configuration files...

For the terraform-docs configuration, I need to decide on the output file location since this is a root-module-only repository.  The output should go to `infra/readme.md`. I'm realizing that `header-from: main.tf` won't work here since we don't have a `main.tf` — the infrastructure is split across `s3.tf`, `cloudfront.tf`, and other files. I could use `providers.tf` instead or just omit the header setting entirely. For the `.pre-commit-config.yaml` terraform_validate hook, I'm checking what paths need to be excluded based on the existing configuration.

The `terraform_validate` hook requires `terraform init` to have been run first, but it doesn't need actual AWS credentials — it just validates syntax and configuration. The pre-commit hook handles initialization with `terraform init -backend=false` internally. Since we don't have modules, there's no need for path exclusions like in the thaler example.

Now I'm ready to write out all the configuration files. For `.secrets.baseline`, I'll generate a fresh baseline since this is a new repository with no existing secrets, setting the timestamp appropriately.

repos:

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v6.0.0
    hooks:
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-added-large-files
        args:
          - '--maxkb=2048'
          - '--enforce-all'
      - id: check-case-conflict
      - id: check-json
      - id: check-yaml
      - id: detect-aws-credentials
        args:
          - '--allow-missing-credentials'
      - id: detect-private-key
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: check-shebang-scripts-are-executable

  - repo: https://github.com/adrienverge/yamllint.git
    rev: v1.37.1
    hooks:
      - id: yamllint
        args: [-c=./.config/.yamllint.yaml]

  - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
    rev: v1.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-secrets
        args: ['--baseline', '.config/.secrets.baseline']
        exclude: .config/.secrets.baseline

  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.98.1
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1

      - id: terraform_validate
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1

      - id: terraform_docs
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - '--args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.terraform-docs.yml'

      - id: terraform_tflint
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1
          - --hook-config=--delegate-chdir
          - '--args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.tflint.hcl'

      - id: terraform_trivy
        files: ^infra/
        args:
          - --hook-config=--parallelism-ci-cpu-cores=1
          - --args=--skip-dirs="**/.terraform"
          - '--args=--ignorefile=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.config/.trivyignore'

  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.0
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks

  - repo: https://github.com/bridgecrewio/checkov.git
    rev: 3.2.266
    hooks:
      - id: checkov
        args:
          - '-d infra'
          - '--config-file=.config/.checkov.yaml'

# gh-ship: Branch → Commit → PR → CI → Merge → CI

> **GitHub repos only.** Before doing anything, confirm the repo has a GitHub remote (`git remote -v`).
> If the remote is Azure DevOps or anything other than `github.com`, stop and tell the user this command does not apply — use the ADO MCP tools instead.

Ship the current working changes end-to-end on GitHub. Run each phase in order, stopping if anything fails.

## Phase 1: Branch

Check current branch with `git branch --show-current`.

- If on `main` or `master`: create a feature branch. Derive the name from the staged changes or `` if provided. Use kebab-case, prefixed with `fix/` or `feat/` per commit convention. Ask if unclear.
- If already on a feature branch: continue.

## Phase 2: Commit

Run `git status` and `git diff --cached` to understand what is staged.

- If nothing is staged: run `git diff --stat` to show unstaged changes, then ask which files to stage.
- Stage and commit following the project commit convention (semantic: `fix:`, `feat:`, `breaking:`; subject ≤50 chars; bullet body with emojis). Do not skip pre-commit hooks (`--no-verify` is forbidden unless the user explicitly requests it).
- If pre-commit hooks modify files, re-stage and commit again.

## Phase 3: Push & PR

Push the branch: `git push -u origin <branch>`.

Create the PR using `gh pr create`. Follow the project PR format:
- Short title (≤70 chars)
- Body with `## Summary` bullets and no test plan section
- Add `🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code)` footer

Show the PR URL.

## Phase 4: CI Check (post-PR)

Wait for CI to complete: `gh run list --branch <branch> --limit 5` then watch the most recent run with `gh run watch <run-id>`.

- If CI passes: proceed to Phase 5.
- If CI fails: show the failed job logs (`gh run view <run-id> --log-failed`), diagnose, fix, commit, push. Re-run Phase 4.

## Phase 5: Merge

Once CI is green: `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch` (use squash unless the user specifies otherwise).

Confirm the merge completed and the branch was deleted.

## Phase 6: CI Check (post-merge)

Run `gh run list --branch main --limit 3` and watch the post-merge run.

- If CI passes: done. Report the merged PR URL and final status.
- If CI fails: report the failure with logs. Do not attempt auto-fix on main — flag it for the user.

## Notes

- `` can provide a branch name, PR title hint, or both.
- Never force-push, never skip hooks, never push directly to main/master.
- If at any point the user needs to take a manual action, stop and clearly state what is needed.

Ah, I think the gitignore in thaler might only ignore root-level or has a different pattern. Or the files were committed before the gitignore rule was added (using `git add -f`). In thaler, the lock file at `infra/.terraform.lock.hcl` is committed while the `.terraform/` directory is ignored.

But the lock files in the modules directory are still committed—they must have been tracked before the gitignore rule was put in place, so Git continues to track them even though the pattern says to ignore them.

For our repo, since the lock file has never been committed, the gitignore rule prevents it from being added. I should either remove the `.terraform.lock.hcl` line from gitignore or force-add the file. Given that lock files are important for reproducible builds, committing it makes sense.

input.command:
gh pr create \
  --title "feat: add holding page infrastructure and tooling" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- 🏗️ Terraform root module — CloudFront + S3 origin + ACM cert + Route 53 for `listell.co.uk` holding page
- 📦 Import blocks to adopt all existing manually-provisioned AWS resources into state
- 🔒 Security hardening: OAC-scoped S3 bucket policy, AES256 SSE, public access blocked
- 🚀 GitHub Actions workflow: lint → plan on PR, lint → apply on merge to main
- 🔍 Pre-commit suite: `terraform fmt/validate/docs/tflint/trivy/checkov`, `gitleaks`, `detect-secrets`, `yamllint`
- ⚙️ Config files: `.tflint.hcl`, `.checkov.yaml`, `.trivyignore`, `.yamllint.yaml`, `.terraform-docs.yml`, secrets baseline
- 🔄 Renovate: Terraform providers + GitHub Actions auto-merge for minor/patch
- 🛠️ Makefile, `.gitignore`, `.vscode/settings.json`
- 🌐 GitHub profile README with stack badges and stats card

> **Note:** The `listell-github-actions` IAM OIDC role and `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` repository secret must exist before the workflow can authenticate to AWS.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.thinking:
The lint job passed, but the plan job failed at OIDC authentication. The error is "Could not assume role with OIDC: Request ARN is invalid". This is expected — the `listell-github-actions` IAM role doesn't exist yet, and the `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` secret hasn't been configured. I mentioned this in the PR description.

I should report this to the user and explain what's needed. The two manual steps required before this will work:
1. Create the `listell-github-actions` IAM role in AWS with proper OIDC trust policy
2. Set the `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` repository secret on GitHub


The plan/apply failure is purely an infrastructure prerequisite issue, not a code problem. Since I can't create IAM roles or set GitHub secrets, and the lint job validated everything correctly, the merge should be fine despite this expected CI failure.

This is the prerequisite I flagged in the PR description: the `listell-github-actions` IAM role doesn't exist yet and `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` isn't set as a repository secret. The plan/apply jobs can't authenticate until that's in place.

The code itself is correct — lint, tflint, trivy, and checkov all pass clean. **This requires two manual steps before CI can go green:**

**1. Create the IAM OIDC role in AWS** — trust policy allowing `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` for `repo:listellm/listellm:*`. Suggest managing this via Terraform in `infra/` (similar pattern to what thaler likely has in their bootstrap).

**2. Set the repository secret** — in GitHub repo Settings → Secrets → `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` = `552644939129`.

- **b)** Merge as-is (lint is green, the auth failure is a known prereq) and address the role separately?

2. `aws_cloudfront_distribution` + `aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control` → CloudFront


3. `aws_acm_certificate` + `aws_acm_certificate_validation` → ACM (us-east-1)
4. `aws_route53_record` (data source + records) → Route53
5. S3 state backend (read/write state, lock file) → S3

Now I need to map out the specific IAM actions required for each service: S3 operations like bucket creation and object management, CloudFront distribution and OAC management, ACM certificate operations, and Route53 record management. I'll scope permissions to the exact resources where possible to keep this minimal.

For the state backend, I need read, write, and delete permissions on the state bucket itself, plus list access. The origin bucket will need broader management capabilities. CloudFront requires distribution and OAC permissions, ACM needs certificate management in us-east-1, and Route53 needs to manage records in the specific hosted zone. Let me work through the exact actions needed for each component, starting with the state backend permissions. Continuing with the S3 bucket permissions, I need to account for all the object operations and bucket attribute reads that Terraform will require. For CloudFront, I'm mapping out the distribution management permissions along with origin access control configuration and tag operations.

Now I'm looking at the ACM certificate operations—describing, listing, requesting, and tagging certificates—plus the Route53 hosted zone and DNS record management. The complexity is mounting with all these service-level permissions, so I'm thinking through a more practical approach using service-level wildcards scoped to specific resource ARNs rather than full admin access.

For the global services like CloudFront and the zone-specific Route53 resources, I can narrow the scope reasonably. Let me draft a policy that covers the Terraform state backend in S3 with separate statements for state bucket listing and object operations...

Writing IAM policy... Finishing the ACM policy and moving into Route53 permissions, which I'm scoping to specific actions like hosted zone management and record set changes rather than blanket service access. I'm thinking through the tradeoff between being overly restrictive with specific ARNs versus keeping enough flexibility for future infrastructure changes. For a personal project, I'll scope S3 to specific buckets but use service-level wildcards for CloudFront and ACM since those resources are more fluid, while keeping Route53 zone-scoped where feasible. input.command:
aws iam put-role-policy \
  --role-name listell-github-actions \
  --policy-name listell-holding-page \
  --policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
      {
        "Sid": "TerraformStateBucket",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": [
          "s3:ListBucket",
          "s3:GetBucketVersioning"
        ],
        "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-terraform-state"
      },
      {
        "Sid": "TerraformStateObjects",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": [
          "s3:GetObject",
          "s3:PutObject",
          "s3:DeleteObject"
        ],
        "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-terraform-state/holding-page/*"
      },
      {
        "Sid": "S3OriginBucket",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": "s3:*",
        "Resource": [
          "arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-origin",
          "arn:aws:s3:::listell-co-uk-origin/*"
        ]
      },
      {
        "Sid": "CloudFront",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": [
          "cloudfront:GetDistribution",
          "cloudfront:GetDistributionConfig",
          "cloudfront:CreateDistribution",
          "cloudfront:UpdateDistribution",
          "cloudfront:DeleteDistribution",
          "cloudfront:TagResource",
          "cloudfront:UntagResource",
          "cloudfront:ListTagsForResource",
          "cloudfront:GetOriginAccessControl",
          "cloudfront:GetOriginAccessControlConfig",
          "cloudfront:CreateOriginAccessControl",
          "cloudfront:UpdateOriginAccessControl",
          "cloudfront:DeleteOriginAccessControl",
          "cloudfront:ListOriginAccessControls",
          "cloudfront:GetCachePolicy",
          "cloudfront:ListCachePolicies"
        ],
        "Resource": "*"
      },
      {
        "Sid": "ACM",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": [
          "acm:DescribeCertificate",
          "acm:ListCertificates",
          "acm:RequestCertificate",
          "acm:DeleteCertificate",
          "acm:AddTagsToCertificate",
          "acm:RemoveTagsFromCertificate",
          "acm:ListTagsForCertificate"
        ],
        "Resource": "*"
      },
      {
        "Sid": "Route53Records",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": [
          "route53:ListHostedZones",
          "route53:ListHostedZonesByName",
          "route53:GetHostedZone",
          "route53:ListResourceRecordSets",
          "route53:ChangeResourceRecordSets",
          "route53:ListTagsForResource"
        ],
        "Resource": [
          "arn:aws:route53:::hostedzone/Z0437334HZSV61L914JE"
        ]
      },
      {
        "Sid": "Route53ChangeStatus",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": "route53:GetChange",
        "Resource": "arn:aws:route53:::change/*"
      }
    ]
  }' 2>&1 && echo "policy attached"
input.description: Attach scoped inline policy covering only the resources in our infra

content.content: policy attached

content.text:
Good. Role is now scoped to exactly what the infra needs:

| Sid | Scope |
|---|---|
| `TerraformStateBucket` | list/versioning on the state bucket |
| `TerraformStateObjects` | read/write/delete the state key only |
| `S3OriginBucket` | full S3 on the origin bucket only |
| `CloudFront` | distribution + OAC + cache policy reads — no wildcards beyond CloudFront service |
| `ACM` | certificate lifecycle only |
| `Route53Records` | record changes scoped to zone `Z0437334HZSV61L914JE` |
| `Route53ChangeStatus` | `GetChange` needed to poll propagation |

Now the `AWS_ACCOUNT_ID` secret — do you want to set that yourself via the GitHub UI, or are you happy for me to do it with `gh secret set`?
